entries.title: Border Radius Scale
entries.description:
## Rule

All border-radius values MUST use only the following scale:

| Token | Value | Use Case |
|-------|-------|----------|
| radius-none | **0px** | Data tables, code blocks, sharp-edged containers |
| radius-sm | **4px** | Subtle rounding: inputs, badges, tooltips, chips |
| radius-md | **8px** | Prominent rounding: cards, modals, dropdowns, buttons |
| radius-full | **9999px** | Circular elements only: avatars, status dots, icon badges |

### Usage Rules

- **0px** (`radius-none`): Required for data tables (`<table>`), code blocks (`<pre>`, `<code>`), and any element where sharp edges communicate precision or structure.
- **4px** (`radius-sm`): Default for inline and small interactive elements -- text inputs, select dropdowns, badges, tags, tooltips.
- **8px** (`radius-md`): Default for container and overlay elements -- cards, dialogs, modals, popovers, dropdown menus, buttons.
- **9999px** (`radius-full`): Restricted to inherently circular or pill-shaped elements: user avatars, status indicator dots, and small icon badges. Never apply to rectangular content containers, cards, or buttons (use `radius-md` for pill-shaped buttons if needed).

### Forbidden

- Arbitrary values: `2px`, `3px`, `6px`, `10px`, `12px`, `16px`, `20px`, `24px`.
- Using `rounded-full` on non-avatar, non-badge elements (cards, modals, containers).
- Mixing different radii on the same element (e.g., `border-radius: 4px 8px 4px 8px`).

## Why

1. **Visual language** -- Consistent corner rounding creates a recognizable shape language. When everything uses the same radii, the interface feels cohesive and intentional.
2. **Hierarchy signaling** -- The two primary values (4px and 8px) create a subtle hierarchy: smaller elements get less rounding, larger containers get more. This mapping helps users distinguish interactive controls from content containers.
3. **Avoiding the "pillow" effect** -- Unrestricted border-radius leads to over-rounded interfaces where elements look inflated and boundaries between components become ambiguous.
4. **Design-to-code fidelity** -- A constrained set of values eliminates guesswork when translating designs to code and prevents per-component radius drift.

## Assertions

- No `border-radius` property may resolve to a value outside `[0, 4, 8, 9999]` px.
- Elements with `radius-full` (9999px) must be one of: avatar, status dot, or icon badge.
- Data table elements (`<table>`, `<thead>`, `<tbody>`, `<tr>`, `<td>`, `<th>`) must have `border-radius: 0`.
- Code block elements (`<pre>`, `<code>`) must have `border-radius: 0`.
- No element may have mixed corner radii (all four corners must use the same value).

entries.title: Loading States
entries.description:
## Rule

Loading indicators MUST use the correct pattern based on what is loading:

| Context | Pattern | Example |
|---------|---------|---------|
| **Content areas** (pages, lists, cards, feeds, dashboards) | **Skeleton loader** | Gray placeholder shapes matching the expected layout |
| **User-initiated actions** (button click, form submit, toggle) | **Spinner** | Inline spinner within the triggering element |

### Skeleton Loaders (Content)

- Skeleton shapes MUST approximate the dimensions and position of the content they replace: a text line skeleton should be the width and height of a text line, an avatar skeleton should be a circle the size of the avatar.
- Use a subtle shimmer animation (left-to-right gradient sweep) to indicate activity. Static gray blocks feel broken.
- Color: `bg-skeleton` token (typically 10-15% opacity of the text color on the surface background).
- Do not show skeleton state for longer than 10 seconds. If loading exceeds 10 seconds, transition to an explicit "still loading" message with a retry option.
- Remove skeleton elements individually as content loads (progressive reveal), not all at once.

### Spinners (Actions)

- Use spinners **only** for discrete user actions: button submissions, toggle switches, inline saves.
- The spinner replaces the element's label/icon while maintaining the element's dimensions (no layout shift).
- Set `aria-busy="true"` on the element or region while loading.
- Spinner size: 16px for inline/button use, 24px for standalone section loaders.
- Spinner color: inherit from the parent element's text color token.

### Forbidden Patterns

- **Full-page spinners**: Never block the entire viewport with a centered spinner. Use skeleton loaders for page-level content.
- **Spinners for content areas**: A spinner in the middle of a card grid or feed provides no spatial context for what will load.
- **No loading indicator**: Content that takes > 300ms to appear must have a loading indicator. Below 300ms, no indicator is needed (avoid flicker).
- **Progress bars for indeterminate loads**: Do not show a progress bar unless you can report real completion percentage.

### Timing

- Delay showing any loading indicator by **300ms**. If the content arrives within 300ms, skip the indicator entirely to avoid a flash.
- After 300ms, show the skeleton or spinner immediately (no fade-in delay).

## Why

1. **Perceived performance** -- Skeleton loaders make the interface feel faster because the user sees a structural preview of the page. Research (Luke Wroblewski, Google) shows skeleton screens reduce perceived wait time by up to 35% compared to spinners.
2. **Spatial expectation** -- Skeletons set spatial expectations: the user knows where content will appear, so the page feels stable when content fills in. Spinners provide no spatial information.
3. **Action feedback** -- Spinners within buttons and toggles give immediate feedback that the user's action was received, preventing duplicate submissions.
4. **Layout stability** -- Skeletons that match content dimensions prevent cumulative layout shift (CLS), which improves both user experience and Core Web Vitals scores.

## Assertions

- Content areas (page sections, card grids, lists, feeds) must use skeleton loaders, not spinners.
- Button and form-submit loading states must use inline spinners, not skeleton loaders.
- No full-page spinner overlay may be used as a loading pattern.
- Skeleton shapes must approximate the dimensions of the content they replace.
- Loading indicators must not appear for loads completing in < 300ms.
- Spinner elements must set `aria-busy="true"` on the loading region.
- Skeleton color must use the `bg-skeleton` design token or equivalent.
- Loading spinner must not cause layout shift (parent element must maintain its dimensions).

entries.title: Typography Scale
entries.description:
## Rule All typographic sizing MUST use only the following three-tier scale: | Token | Font Size | Use Case | |----------|-----------|----------| | text-sm | **14px** (0.875rem) | Captions, helper text, metadata, labels | | text-md | **16px** (1rem) | Body text, form inputs, list items | | text-lg | **20px** (1.25rem) | Section headings, card titles, emphasis | No other font sizes are permitted in the component library. ### Line Height | Context | Line Height | |---------|-------------| | Body text (text-sm, text-md) | **1.5** (150%) | | Headings (text-lg) | **1.2** (120%) | ### Font Weight Only three weights are allowed: | Token | Weight | Use Case | |-------|--------|----------| | font-regular | **400** | Body text, descriptions, helper text | | font-semibold | **600** | Labels, subheadings, emphasis within body | | font-bold | **700** | Primary headings, strong callouts | ### Forbidden - Font sizes outside `[14, 16, 20]` px (e.g., 10px, 12px, 13px, 18px, 24px, 32px). - Font weights outside `[400, 600, 700]` (e.g., 300 light, 500 medium, 800 extrabold). - Line heights below 1.2 or arbitrary values like 1.3, 1.4, 1.8. ## Why 1. **Visual hierarchy with restraint** -- Three sizes are sufficient to establish a clear hierarchy (primary, secondary, tertiary) without visual clutter. More sizes dilute the hierarchy and force users to decode subtle differences. 2. **Readability** -- A 1.5 line height for body text ensures comfortable reading across languages and screen sizes. A 1.2 line height for headings keeps them compact and distinct from body text. 3. **Consistency** -- Limiting weights to three prevents the "font-weight soup" where 300, 400, 500, 600, 700 all appear in the same view with no discernible logic. 4. **Performance** -- Fewer font variations mean fewer font files to load, improving page load times and reducing layout shifts. ## Assertions - No `font-size` property may resolve to a value outside `[14, 16, 20]` px or their rem equivalents `[0.875, 1, 1.25]`. - No `font-weight` property may resolve to a value outside `[400, 600, 700]`. - Body text elements (`

`, ``, `

  • `, ``) must have `line-height` of `1.5`. - Heading elements and tokens using text-lg must have `line-height` of `1.2`. - No `font-size` may use absolute keywords (`x-small`, `small`, `large`, `x-large`).
